title: How To Aspirate Without Causing Trauma
filename: How To Aspirate Without Causing Trauma.en.vtt
excerpt: If I pull with this hand against this hand you can see that I'm it's gonna be very hard for me to maintain complete neutrality across the syringe so tell us about aspirating how do you do that you need to have a stable patient stable hand it's all back down to that stability if a…

segments.text: if I pull with this hand against this hand you can see that I'm it's gonna be very hard for me to maintain complete neutrality across the syringe so tell us about aspirating how do you do that you need to have a stable patient stable hand it's all back down to that stability if all of this is locked in place so I'm stable at my wrist I'm stabilized my fingers even the only bit that's moving here I'm actually resting in the case of a periosteal injection you've got one more place to rest which is on the patient's bone basically sounds terrible it didn't actually hurt that much and then you aspirate that you can see that needle tip is not moving much at all so that's a meaningful aspiration then I can inject and I know that at the point I've aspirated I can inject more safely than without aspirating I can say it that way so the other thing you need to practice is how are you actually going to aspirate and this there's a lot of practice and many different ways of doing it one of the key things I've taught us before in different videos is you need to find a way of aspirating that has a

neutral vector across the syringe and what I mean by that is if I pull with this hand against this hand you can see that I'm it's going to be very hard for me to maintain complete new child neutrality across the syringe because one hand is likely to be slightly stronger than the other and it will move the tip of the needle so you want to find a way of doing it the easiest way is with one hand if that if I'm pulling a thumb out from these fingers the force that is opposing the thumb is the fingers so that the net force is zero it's very I can't pull my hand apart my thumb's gonna fly off in a different direction so this is one way that you aspirate in a way that makes the net force across the syringe zero so using the same hand as one way to do that and in fact it's the way you do with this side as well if you're gonna aspirate using your left hand and stabilize with your right hand you still do the same thing you push with one finger and pull with the other finger as soon as you do that which is what the most new injectors do they do that you've now got your stabilizing coins the middle of

your body is really likely you're going to show some imperfections there and move positioned and that's really frustrating you haven't got a good aspiration technique the argument that it caused more drama is probably true which is why you need to understand an aspiration technique which is all around the stability and the neutral vector across the syringe that will give you control
